speaker: interviewer
text: '95. Interview with Morris Libfeld. This is tape four. Mr. Libfeld, you were telling us how you had heard that your brother went to Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I didn't find out where. I know that he went someplace, but like I told you, a lot of people used to go to Rome. And there, they got money for converting. My brother had-- after the war, he came home. He took all our pictures what we had buried before we were sent to Germany. And he was holding them with him. He went to Katowice. And he had his passport and all the pictures in his pocket. And they pulled it on him. And this way, he lost all the pictures and everything. And now, we don't have-- we have only one or two pictures. From, from the parents, we don't have any. We only, only have is from my brothers and sister. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I went?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Kielce?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed only close, not even two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went-- during those two weeks, I went to, I went to Lodz. I took from my uncle, from my uncle was in Kielce already. He took over the factory and the house. And he said, you need some money? I said, yeah, sure, I need. So he gave me 100,000 zylots. I took 100,000 zylots, and I bought two tickets to Lodz. I took my friend with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which friend was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died here in Canada, Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Monek [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Monek Laupter. We both went to Lodz. And we were there weeks-- not a week, not even a week. We spent all the money. We didn't have enough money to take, buy a ticket to go back to Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you spend the money on?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? On booze, women, on what? What could you spend a lot of money? And we went back without tickets. We were hiding. When the control went one side, we went the other side. And when we came back to Kielce, I said, that's enough. We're going to see some friends, to Ludwigsdorf, because I heard that from friends are alive and they were liberated close by and they lived there. So we didn't have where to go. It was close to a border with Germany. So I forget, we go. We went there. We came in there. There was one guy, [? Hansel ?] [? Gunter. ?] And he passed away about close to a year ago. He said to some of the boys, go to the Burgermeister. You know what the Burgermeister is. They used to go to the Burgermeister to take some coupons for food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I look at my friend, and he looks at me. He says, that's what we came here for? They should wait until we get some stamps for food? I said, that's not our-- our doing. We don't know that life. So we didn't have no train to go back right away. The next day, we had a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, in the night, in the evening, we went for a walk, some boys and girls. We walked by. I see all kind of chicken, goose, all kind of turkeys. I said to one of the guys who lived there, I said, you guys are starving. You haven't got no food. And here, they have all kind of, all kind of fowl for you. He said, yeah, that's why, that's why we live here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I sent a couple of guys for sacks, to bring big sacks. And we went into a couple places. And we took out three sacks of all kind of-- the Germans were afraid at that time to come out and, and say anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: We took it out. We took it over to the house. The next morning, the women started to cook. So we had food. We stayed there about three, maybe four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we went back to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Kielce. Four days later, we went back there. We came there. And we asked, guys, how far is the border to Germany? He says, the border is about three miles away, where we used to-- where they used to live. I said, and nobody ever went over there? He says, no. We're keeping that border for later when we have to go, and we want to go to Germany. We'll go through that border. We had to go to-- to go to Germany, we had to go through, not Italy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Wait a minute. It was, it was Italy. No. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went through to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: When--

speaker: interviewer
text: You eventually went through and crossed the border into Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to border there. About three, four days later, we crossed the border. And we went to Germany. In Germany, a friend of ours lived in Pasel. So we figured, might as well go to Pasel. It's not far. It was only about 40 miles. So we went to Pasel. There was a friend of mine. He said, you stay here. You don't go any farther. So we stayed for maybe a week. And then somebody came. And he said, that a friend of ours, it was my, my friend [INAUDIBLE]. He is now in New York. He lives in New York. He's a doctor. He says, he want-- he wants to get out of Poland. He's afraid. He was working at the, at the [NON-ENGLISH]. That's like the income there, in Poland. He's afraid because in the next room from him was a friend of his. And they came in. And they killed him right there by the, by his desk.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. The Polacks killed him there. So he was afraid to stay. He was a captain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I knew?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure I knew him from, I knew him from camp. I was together. A matter of fact, from camp, he didn't stay in camp to be liberated. He ran away from camp with another friend and a girl. The three of them, they went to the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you reconnected with this man after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, now I go to Chicago, I see him. He was doctor-- he was a woman doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernard-- like Bernard, Bernie, [? what. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure you'll think of it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you reconnected with him and made arrangements with him to go to the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I made arrangements, I made arrangements with him that when we-- when he was, he was ready to go with us. But he wouldn't go by himself. He was still in the army, in the, in the [NON-ENGLISH]. So we made up that this and this day, he's going to come to Ludwigsdorf, because there we had to cross the border. In Ludwigsdorf, he's going to change his clothes, and throw away his gun with everything, and come with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Germany. It had, that's the way it happened. He came to Ludwigsdorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in Germany then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, I stayed there over four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pasel. I didn't stay in Pasel, but I was all over Germany. I was traveling around. We had a car.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Black market.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after your four years in Germany, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: After four years in Germany, I went to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Little Rock, Arkansas, where our president is now.

speaker: interviewer
text: By this time, you were married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I married in 1943, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the girl you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose [? Madlogs ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you married Rose, and you came to Little Rock, Arkansas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived there for 18 or 19 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in Arkansas?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked as a floor sander. I started to work for $20 a week, 84 hours. How much is it an hour?

speaker: interviewer
text: And after living in Arkansas, you decided to move to Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: After my wife got pregnant, I decided to move back. Because in the meantime, I got connections with some friends from Chicago. And we figured, we have got nothing to lose in here. So we went by car. We put everything into the car, and we traveled to Chicago. We came to Chicago. We stopped by my friend. We slept there. I went to work as a carpenter. I was working at a big store they were remodeling. I was working three days and three nights. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I got paid about close to $400. So it was a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you think about your experiences in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: How often I thought? In the beginning, I thought a lot. Because I thought, I never forget-- forget about it. But when the time passed by, I was trying not to, not to think about it. Because I knew that nothing, I can nothing bring back. So I was set out to go straight and not to think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: After having experienced the camps, did that affect the way you believe in God, or affect the way you were brought up with regard to religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: It affects-- it affected my way. The only way it affected is how am I was born to and raised to a Jewish family? And my father was orthodox. He didn't-- he wouldn't take money from you on Saturday. He wasn't-- he didn't wear religious clothing. But he himself, he and my mother, they were religious people. We kids were raised up half and half, because when we got older, we used to run whatever we felt like, not as we were told at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: But after having experienced what you did, did that affect the way you believe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was it your belief in God that sustained you through those hard times?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I, I, after I went through all this, I lost mine believing religion. Even now, I don't believe in any, anything a religion would tell you. The only thing I believe is in honesty and be a person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Libfeld, why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I survived? I don't know. Maybe some part, it was that I was young and strong. Nothing could keep me back from anything. And maybe it was-- it was mine-- my number didn't come up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your experiences affected the way you brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The experiences, partial. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I brought up my children to be honest. Should never tell a lie, just like my wife was the same way. And do whatever they can, but honest way. And that was my, my main goal to teach the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to give us this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Please?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to do this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I wanted to do an interview? I don't know. Maybe it's going to be left something to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a message that you'd like to leave to your children or your grandchildren, the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: A message? The only message I could give them is to be a person and be honest. And the rest of the world thinks it comes by itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for allowing us to interview you. And now, I believe you have some pictures you want to share with us, and introduce us to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the-- I don't know. My wife prepared all the pictures. She, she showed you all her pictures. So if she shows you, it's all within the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture taken in Poland before the war. That's my sister, my older sister, to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the middle is my older brother. And to the right is my older bro-- oldest brother. The name says Avraham, meyer. And my sister's name was [? Helen. ?] This is my sister's picture and my uncle's sister, Helen Libfeld, and a cousin of my uncle from [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your sister seated or standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was Helen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is she? On the left or the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, was standing. My sister's on the right, standing. This is my picture from myself and my wife, Rose Libfeld. But maiden name was Rose [? Mandelhans, ?] friends and family at my wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1947. This is the picture my grandson's bar mitzvah this year in February. On the left is, is my son-in-law, Jeff, and his daughter, Sarah, my daughter, Gloria, and son, Brian, my wife, Rose, myself, and my grandson, my grandson, Steven, my daughter-in-law, Sheila, my granddaughter, Rachel, and my son, Al.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, this is my whole family, from my, my wife, Rose, my son, Al, my daughter-in-law, Sheila, my grandson, Steven, and my granddaughter, Rachel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is-- sorry we could not get mine-- our daughter, Gloria. our daughter, Gloria, and Jeff, Brian, and Sarah. We love you, but we miss you. And we love you all here very much. Just be well. We should have you a long time, and you should have us.
